seoTitle: How to Embed Audio From Google Drive Into Google Docs
metaDescription: Learn how to link playable audio files from Google Drive into Google Docs using smart chips or clickable text and images.
intro: Google Docs doesn't have a native audio player, but you can still give readers access to audio files stored in Google Drive using a few structural workarounds. This guide covers setting the right sharing permissions, using Smart Chips for a clean file preview, and linking audio over text or an image for an intuitive playback experience.

steps.heading: Set sharing permissions
steps.body: In Google Drive, open your audio file's share settings and set access to anyone with the link.

steps.heading: Copy the share link
steps.body: Copy the audio file's live share URL from Drive.

steps.heading: Paste it for a Smart Chip
steps.body: In Google Docs, paste the link and press Tab to generate an automated Smart Chip preview of the file.

steps.heading: Or link it over text
steps.body: Alternatively, highlight descriptive text in your document and bind the audio link to it using the Insert Link tool.

steps.heading: Or use a play button image
steps.body: Insert a play button graphic and layer a clickable link over the image to simulate an audio player.

steps.heading: Test the link
steps.body: Click the link to confirm the audio file opens and plays cleanly in a browser tab before sharing the document.

faq.question: Does Google Docs have a built-in audio player?
faq.answer: No, Google Docs doesn't include a native audio player icon, so linking to a file in Drive is the standard workaround.

faq.question: What sharing setting do I need for the audio to play for others?
faq.answer: You need to set the file's access to anyone with the link so recipients don't hit a permissions error when clicking through.

faq.question: What is a Smart Chip in Google Docs?
faq.answer: It's an automated preview element that Google Docs generates when you paste a Drive link and press Tab, showing a clean file reference instead of a raw URL.

faq.question: Can I make an image look like a play button that links to the audio?
faq.answer: Yes, inserting a play button image and layering a clickable link over it is a common way to visually simulate an audio player in the document.
